title.emphasis: Section II: Systematic Analysis of State Jurisdictions

section.para: The following is a comprehensive state-by-state documentation of the legal frameworks governing sexual consent. This analysis is based on a systematic review of statutory language and legal interpretations from the provided research materials, presented objectively and without commentary.

title.emphasis: Table 1: Comparative Overview of State Sexual Consent Laws

row.entry: Higher force standard; lesser penalty; time limit
Deferred prosecution option for spousal rape

listitem.para: Statutory Definition of Consent: Alabama law does not provide a direct statutory definition of "consent." Instead, its legal framework is coercion-based, defining a "lack of consent" as resulting from "forcible compulsion" or the victim "being incapable of consent".8 Case law has interpreted consent to mean "acquiescence or compliance".8 The law permits a defense of implied consent if a defendant can argue an "honest and reasonable belief" that consent was given based on the accuser's conduct.8
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions unique to Alabama.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Alaska statute provides a consent-based, affirmative definition. "Without consent" means the absence of a "freely given, reversible agreement specific to the conduct at issue," where "freely given" means agreement was "positively expressed by word or action".21
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Alaska.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Arizona statute does not define "consent" but defines sexual assault as sexual conduct "without consent of the other person".25 The law further specifies that "without consent" includes situations of coercion by force or threat, or when the victim is incapable of consenting due to impairment, a condition that the defendant knew or should have known.26 This creates a hybrid model that focuses on the absence of consent but defines it primarily through coercion and incapacity.
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Arizona.1
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Arkansas law does not provide a direct statutory definition of "consent." Its legal framework is coercion-based, with a lack of consent resulting from "forcible compulsion" or the victim's incapacity (e.g., being physically helpless, mentally defective, or mentally incapacitated).27
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Arkansas.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: California Penal Code § 261.6 provides a clear affirmative consent model, defining consent as "positive cooperation in act or attitude pursuant to the exercise of free will." The law specifies that consent must be affirmative, conscious, and voluntary, and that silence, passivity, or a lack of resistance does not constitute consent.15

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. California law previously contained an exemption where a spouse could not be found guilty of raping a victim who was unable to consent due to a mental or physical disability. This exemption was repealed by Assembly Bill 1171, and the law now requires the same standards for spousal and non-spousal rape.1
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Colorado Revised Statute § 18-3-401 provides a consent-based definition, stating that consent means "cooperation in act or attitude pursuant to an exercise of free will and with knowledge of the nature of the act." The statute clarifies that a prior relationship does not constitute consent and submission under fear is not consent.32

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The statute notes that a crime can occur if a victim submits erroneously believing the actor is their spouse, but it does not provide a general marital exemption.32 The provided materials do not identify other specific statutory exemptions for Colorado.1
Age of Consent: 17.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Connecticut law does not provide a direct statutory definition of "consent." Its legal framework is coercion-based, defining a lack of consent as existing where the accused compels the victim by use or threat of force, or where the victim is mentally incapacitated or physically helpless.33

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, the law provides an affirmative defense for certain sexual crimes if the defendant and alleged victim were "living together by mutual consent in a relationship of cohabitation, regardless of marriage" at the time of the offense.33 This creates a significant legal distinction for cohabiting partners, including spouses. The state is also listed as one of 12 with a marital rape loophole.34
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Delaware law does not provide a direct statutory definition of "consent." The legal framework for adult sexual assault is primarily coercion-based, criminalizing acts where a victim is compelled to submit by force or threat.21 The statutes are heavily structured around age-based offenses and incapacity.35

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. A narrow marital exemption exists in the statutory rape law, where marriage is a defense if the victim is 16 or 17 and the defendant is 30 or older.35 The provided materials do not identify broader statutory exemptions.1
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Florida Statute § 794.011 provides a consent-based definition, stating that consent is "intelligent, knowing, and voluntary consent and does not include coerced submission." The law explicitly clarifies that a failure to offer physical resistance does not constitute consent.36
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Florida.1
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Georgia. The statutory rape law (Georgia Code § 16-6-3) is based on the victim's age, not their consent.38 This indicates a coercion-based model for other sexual offenses.

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The statutory rape law explicitly applies to sexual intercourse with a person who is "not his or her spouse," but this provision is specific to underage victims.38 The provided materials do not identify broader statutory exemptions for adult spouses.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Hawaii's laws operate on a consent-based model. The statutes are framed around nonconsensual sexual conduct, with "compulsion" defined as the "absence of consent" or a threat.39

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The statutory rape provision for minors between 14 and 16 includes an exception if the offender is married to the minor, implying marriage can be a defense in this specific age-based context.39 The provided materials do not identify broader statutory exemptions for adult spouses.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Idaho law does not specifically define "consent." Its rape statute (Idaho Statutes § 18-6101) is coercion-based, listing circumstances under which penetration is a crime, including when the victim is incapable of giving legal consent, resistance is overcome by force, or the victim is unconscious or intoxicated.40

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, the statutory rape provisions for victims under 16 or between 16-17 explicitly state they do not apply if "the victim is lawfully married to the perpetrator".40 This creates a clear marital exemption for age-based offenses. Idaho is also listed among the 12 states with a marital rape loophole.34
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Illinois law (720 ILCS 5/11-1.70) provides an affirmative consent model, defining consent as "a freely given agreement to the act of sexual penetration or sexual conduct in question." The statute specifies that lack of resistance due to force or threat does not constitute consent and that consent can be withdrawn at any time.42
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Illinois.1
Age of Consent: 17.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Indiana. The law cited (Indiana Code 35-42-4-9) pertains to sexual misconduct with a minor and is based on age, not consent, indicating a likely coercion-based model for other sexual offenses.43
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Indiana.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: While the state code is not provided directly, a university policy based on Iowa law reflects an affirmative consent model, defining consent as "clear, knowing, and voluntary" and "active, not passive".44
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. Iowa is listed among the 12 states with a marital rape loophole.34
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Kansas law does not specifically define "consent." Its rape statute (K.S.A. 21-5503) is coercion-based, criminalizing intercourse where the victim "does not consent" under circumstances of force, fear, or incapacity.9

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, the law provides a defense to the rape or sodomy of a child under 14 if "the child was married to the accused at the time of the offense".9 This is a clear marital exemption for specific age-based offenses.
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Kentucky. The sexual abuse statute (KRS § 510.110) is coercion-based, criminalizing sexual contact by "forcible compulsion" or with a person "incapable of consent".45
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Kentucky.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Louisiana law does not define "consent." Its legal framework is coercion-based, defining sex crimes based on circumstances where the victim's resistance is overcome by force or threats, or where the victim is incapacitated or underage.11

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, it is a defense to sexual battery with a child under 15 or carnal knowledge of a juvenile that "the actor is the child's spouse".11 This is a clear marital exemption for age-based offenses.
Age of Consent: 17.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Maine law provides an affirmative consent model, defining consent as "a word or action by a person that indicates a freely given agreement".46 The law also emphasizes that consent should be voluntary, ongoing, informed, and enthusiastic.47
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Maine.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Maryland. The rape statute (MD. CRIM LAW § 3-303) is coercion-based, criminalizing intercourse by "force, or the threat of force, without the consent of the other".12
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. Maryland's "spousal defense," which previously required proof of force if the spouses were living together, was repealed in 2023.34
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Massachusetts law does not specifically define "consent." Its legal framework is coercion-based, with the standard being whether the accused compels the victim to submit "by force and against his or her will, or compels such person to submit by threat of bodily injury".10

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Massachusetts.1 The state has no "Romeo and Juliet" law for close-in-age exemptions.1
Age of Consent: 16.3
Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Michigan, indicating a likely coercion-based model.50

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, Michigan law (Section 750.520l) contains a significant loophole: it explicitly exempts from prosecution situations where the criminality of a sexual act is based solely on a spouse being "mentally incapable, or mentally incapacitated".1 This creates a legally permissible scenario for a husband to have non-consensual sex with his incapacitated wife, an act that would be criminal if committed by a non-spouse.
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Minnesota law (MN Stat. § 609.341) provides an affirmative consent model, defining consent as "words or overt actions by a person indicating a freely given present agreement to perform a particular sexual act with another".51
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. Minnesota is listed among the 12 states with a marital rape loophole.34
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Mississippi. The law cited (Mississippi Code § 97-3-65) focuses on "forcible sexual intercourse" and statutory rape, indicating a coercion-based model.53

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, similar to Michigan, Mississippi law provides an exemption for sex with a "mentally incapacitated" spouse. A husband can only be convicted of sexual battery of a spouse with whom he is living if he uses "forcible penetration against the victim's will," which explicitly excludes cases where the victim is incapacitated and force is not used.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Missouri law provides an affirmative consent model, defining consent as a "clear, knowing, and voluntary agreement to engage in sexual activity." It must be an affirmative agreement, and silence or passivity does not constitute consent.54
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Missouri.1
Age of Consent: 17.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Montana law (MCA 45-5-501) provides an affirmative consent model, defining consent as "words or overt actions indicating a freely given agreement to have sexual intercourse or sexual contact".55

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, the statute contains several exemptions to the "incapable of consent" provisions if the individuals are married. For instance, provisions criminalizing sex between a psychotherapy client and provider, or a student and school employee, do not apply if the parties are married.55
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Nebraska law does not define "consent" but defines "without consent" as occurring through force or coercion, or if the victim verbally or through conduct expresses a lack of consent.57 The law specifies that physical resistance is not required, creating a hybrid model.58
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Nebraska.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Nevada. The law is coercion-based, criminalizing penetration "against the will of the victim" or when the victim is incapable of resisting or understanding.59

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, Nevada law (NRS 200.373) states that marriage is not a defense if the assault was committed by force or threat of force.49 This implies that for non-forcible sexual assault, such as with an incapacitated victim, marriage may serve as a defense. Nevada is listed among the 12 states with a marital rape loophole.34
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for New Hampshire. The law is coercion-based, defining sexual assault as engaging in sexual contact or penetration "without their consent" under circumstances involving force, helplessness, or incapacity.60
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for New Hampshire.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: New Jersey law provides an affirmative consent model, defining consent as an "affirmative, conscious, and voluntary agreement to engage in specific sexual activity." It cannot be assumed from silence or lack of resistance.61
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for New Jersey.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: New Mexico law is coercion-based. The provided materials state that "absence of consent is not an element of the crime of criminal sexual penetration" in the state.62 The laws are structured around age and incapacity.
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, New Mexico provides a marital exemption for statutory rape, allowing consensual sex between a married minor and their spouse.63
Age of Consent: 17.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: New York Penal Law § 130.05 has a hybrid model. "Lack of consent" results from forcible compulsion or incapacity. However, for lesser offenses, it can be inferred from "expressly or impliedly acquiesce in the actor's conduct," which allows a defendant to argue consent was implied by behavior.1

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, the law provides a defense to some sex crimes if the defendant is married to the victim and the crime is based on a lack of consent due to a mental disability or age.30 There are also exemptions for employees of correctional or care facilities if they are married to the person in custody.64
Age of Consent: 17.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: North Carolina law provides a consent-based definition, stating that an act "against the will of the other person" is one done "without the consent of the other person".67
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The law explicitly states that a person may be prosecuted "whether or not the victim is the person's legal spouse".67
Age of Consent: 16.3
Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for North Dakota.68
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for North Dakota.1
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Ohio. The rape statute is coercion-based, structured around force, threat, or the victim's incapacity.69

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The law explicitly states it is not a defense to forcible rape that the parties were married.69 However, Ohio is listed among the 12 states with a marital rape loophole, suggesting exemptions may exist for non-forcible acts, such as with an incapacitated spouse.34
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Oklahoma law provides an affirmative consent model, defining consent as an "affirmative, unambiguous and voluntary agreement to engage in a specific sexual activity" that can be revoked at any time. It cannot be inferred from silence or a prior relationship.71

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime, and the statute explicitly applies "within or without the bonds of matrimony".72 However, Oklahoma is listed among the 12 states with a marital rape loophole.34
Age of Consent: 16.3
Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Oregon.73
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Oregon.1
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Pennsylvania. The laws are structured around age and power imbalances (institutional sexual assault), where consent is deemed impossible, indicating a coercion-based model for other offenses.74
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Pennsylvania.1
Age of Consent: 16.3
Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Rhode Island.75
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. Rhode Island is listed among the 12 states with a marital rape loophole.34
Age of Consent: 16.3
Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for South Carolina. The laws are coercion-based, structured around force and incapacity.76

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime, but South Carolina's laws create significant distinctions. "Spousal sexual battery" requires "aggravated force," a higher standard than for non-spousal rape. The maximum penalty is 10 years, compared to 30 years for non-spousal rape. A 30-day reporting time limit applies if the couple lives together.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for South Dakota. The rape statute is coercion-based, defining the crime based on force, coercion, threats, or the victim's incapacity.77
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for South Dakota.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Tennessee. The rape statute operates on a hybrid model, criminalizing penetration "without the consent of the victim" when the defendant knows or should know consent was absent, or when force is used or the victim is incapacitated.78
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Tennessee.1
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Texas Penal Code § 1.07 provides an ambiguous model, defining consent as "assent in fact, whether express or apparent." However, consent is not effective if induced by force, threat, or fraud, or given by a person known to be unable to make reasonable decisions due to youth, mental defect, or intoxication.79
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. An affirmative defense exists for the sexual assault of a child if "the actor was the spouse of the child at the time of the offense".79
Age of Consent: 17.3
Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Utah.80
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Utah.1
Age of Consent: 18.3
Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Vermont.81
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Vermont.1
Age of Consent: 16.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Virginia. The rape statute is coercion-based, criminalizing intercourse "against the complaining witness's will, by force, threat or intimidation" or through incapacity.82

Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. However, a court has the option to defer prosecution and waive penalties for spousal rape if the defendant completes counseling and the victim agrees. This option is not available for non-spousal rape and can pressure a victim into a lesser outcome.1
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: Washington law (RCW 9A.44.010) provides an affirmative consent model, defining consent as "actual words or conduct indicating freely given agreement to have sexual intercourse or sexual contact".83
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Washington.1
Age of Consent: 16.3
Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for West Virginia.
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for West Virginia.1
Age of Consent: 16.3
Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Wisconsin.
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Wisconsin.1
Age of Consent: 18.3

Statutory Definition of Consent: The provided materials do not contain a statutory definition of consent for Wyoming.84 The sexual assault statute is coercion-based, structured around force, confinement, threats, or the victim's helplessness or incapacity.84
Marital Rape Exemptions: Marital rape is a crime. The provided materials do not identify specific statutory exemptions for Wyoming.1
Age of Consent: 17 or 18, depending on the source.3
